narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like choosing full-time study. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 54% chance of failure for you, which tipped the decision to reject your application to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing, strongly pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, slightly below the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor by suggesting a better shot at passing than the model might otherwise predict. Your LSAT score of 30, well below the dataset average of about 37, also helped pull the prediction toward passing, though not enough to overcome the top concern. Similarly, your Year 1 decile in the bottom group added some concern by nudging toward failure, while your middle family income bracket had a minor influence in that direction, and your full-time enrollment status offered a small boost. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your academic deciles stand out as notably lower, which carried the most weight here despite some counterbalancing factors like your GPA and LSAT. We're rooting for you and encourage focusing on boosting those decile rankings through stronger coursework or targeted prep in future applications. Consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average, as it could shift predictions favorably. Building a stronger undergraduate record or seeking academic support might also help elevate your profile. You have great potential—many in similar situations succeed with persistence, so keep pushing forward. We wish you the best in your next steps toward law school.
